content: If 2y = x and 3y + 4x = 0 are the equations of a pair of conjugate diameters of an ellipse, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
explanation:
2y = x, 3y + 4x = 0 are equations of a pair of conjugate diameters. Let m1, m2 are respective slopes of diameters.

$${m_1} = {1 \over 2}$$, $${m_2} = - {4 \over 3}$$

Since, $${m_1}{m_2} = - {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${1 \over 2} \times - {4 \over 3} = {{ - {b^2}} \over {{a^2}}}$$ or $${{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} = {2 \over 3}$$

$${e^2} = 1 - {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} = 1 - {2 \over 2} = {1 \over 3}$$

eccentricity $$e = \sqrt {{1 \over 3}} $$.

content: The equation of the ellipse having vertices at ($$\pm$$ 5, 0) and foci ($$\pm$$ 4, 0) is
explanation:
Vertices $$\to$$ ($$\pm$$ a, 0) = ($$\pm$$ 5, 0) (given)

$$\therefore$$ a = $$\pm$$ 5 ..... (i)

Foci $$\to$$ ($$\pm$$ ae, 0) = ($$\pm$$ 4, 0) (given)

$$\therefore$$ ae = 4 ...... (ii)

From (i) and (ii), e = 4/5

Now, $${b^2} = {a^2}(1 - {e^2}) = {5^2}\left( {1 - {{\left( {{4 \over 5}} \right)}^2}} \right) = 25 \times {9 \over {25}} = 9$$

$$\therefore$$ b = $$\pm$$ 3

So, equation of ellipse will be

$${{{x^2}} \over {{5^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{3^2}}} = 1 \Rightarrow {{{x^2}} \over {25}} + {{{y^2}} \over 9} = 1 \Rightarrow 9{x^2} + 25{y^2} = 225$$.

content: The latus rectum of an ellipse is equal to one-half of its minor axis. The eccentricity of the ellipse is
explanation:
Latus rectum of ellipse = $${1 \over 2}$$ (minor axis) (given)

$$ \Rightarrow {{2{b^2}} \over a} = {1 \over 2}(2b)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{b^2} = ab \Rightarrow 2b = a$$ ..... (i)

Now, $${b^2} = {a^2}(1 - {e^2})$$

$${{{a^2}} \over 4} = {a^2}(1 - {e^2})$$

$$1 - {e^2} = {1 \over 4} \Rightarrow e = {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2}$$ (as $$-$$ve value of e is not possible)

content: The total number of tangents through the point (3, 5) that can be drawn to the ellipse 3x2 + 5y2 = 32 and 25x2 + 9y2 = 450 is
explanation:
$$\because$$ 3 . 32 + 5 . 52 $$-$$ 32 > 0

$$\therefore$$ From (3, 5) we can draw 2 tangents to the ellipse

3x2 + 5y2 = 32. Again 25 . 32 + 9 . 52 $$-$$ 450 = 0

$$\therefore$$ From (3, 5) we can draw only 1 tangent to the ellipse

25x2 + 9y2 = 450 $$\therefore$$ Required number of tangents = 2 + 1 = 3

content: The line y = 2t2 intersects the ellipse $${{{x^2}} \over 9} + {{{y^2}} \over 4} = 1$$ in real point if
explanation:
Keep y = 2t2 in $${{{x^2}} \over 9} + {{{y^2}} \over 4} = 1$$, we get

$${{{x^2}} \over 9} + {{{{(2{t^2})}^2}} \over 4} = 1 \Rightarrow {x^2} = 9(1 - {t^4})$$

If line intersects the ellipse in real points, then x has real roots

So $${x^2} \ge 0 \Rightarrow 9(1 - {t^4}) \ge 0 \Rightarrow (1 - {t^2})(1 + {t^2}) \ge 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 - {t^2} \ge 0 \Rightarrow |t| \ge 1$$ [$$\because$$ $$1 + {t^2} > 0$$]

content: The angle between the line joining the foci of an ellipse to one particular extremity of the minor axis is 90$$^\circ$$. The eccentricity of the ellipse is
explanation:
Let the equation of the ellipse is $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

coordinates of foci are (ae, 0), ($$-$$ae, 0)

coordinates of extremity of minor axis are (0, b)

Slopes of lines joining foci to extremity are $$ - {b \over {ae}},{b \over {ae}}$$

but lines are at right angle

$$\therefore$$ $$ - {b \over {ae}}.{b \over {ae}} = - 1 \Rightarrow {b^2} = {a^2}{e^2}$$ .... (i)

But $${b^2} = {a^2}(1 - {e^2})$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^2}{e^2} = {a^2}(1 - {e^2}) \Rightarrow 2{e^2} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow e = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$

content: S and T are the foci of an ellipse and B is an end point of the minor axis. If STB is an equilateral triangle, the eccentricity of the ellipse is
explanation:
$$\because$$ $$\Delta$$SBT is equilateral

$$\angle$$BTC = 60$$^\circ$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\tan 60^\circ = {{BC} \over {CT}} = {b \over {ae}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sqrt 3 = {b \over {ae}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {b^2} = 3{a^2}{e^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {a^2}(1 - {e^2}) = 3{a^2}{e^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow e = {1 \over 2}$$

[$$\because$$ $${b^2} = {a^2}(1 - {e^2})$$ in any ellipse $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$]

content: The length of the latus rectum of the ellipse is 16x2 + 25y2 = 400 is
The equation 8x2 + 12y2 $$-$$ 4x + 4y $$-$$ 1 = 0 represents
The equation $$\mathrm{r} \cos \theta=2 \mathrm{a} \sin ^2 \theta$$ represents the curve

A line of fixed length $$\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} . \mathrm{a} \neq \mathrm{b}$$ moves so that its ends are always on two fixed perpendicular straight lines. The locus of a point which divides the line into two parts of length a and b is
With origin as a focus and $$x=4$$ as corresponding directrix, a family of ellipse are drawn. Then the locus of an end of minor axis is

The tangent at point $$(a\cos \theta ,b\sin \theta ),0 < \theta < {\pi \over 2}$$, to the ellipse $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$ meets the x-axis at T and y-axis at T$$_1$$. Then the value of $$\mathop {\min }\limits_{0 < \theta < {\pi \over 2}} (OT)(O{T_1})$$ is
explanation:
Tangent at $$(a \cos \theta, b \sin \theta)$$ to $$\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$$ is

given by $$\frac{x \cos \theta}{a}+\frac{y \sin \theta}{b}=1$$ (parametric form) ..... (i)

Putting, $$y=0 \Rightarrow x=a \sec \theta$$ ..... (ii)

Putting, $$x=0 \Rightarrow y=b \operatorname{cosec} \theta$$ ..... (iii)

So, $$T(a \sec \theta, 0)$$ and $$T_1(0, b \operatorname{cosec} \theta)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, }(0, T)\left(0, T_1\right)=\frac{a}{|\cos \theta|} \times \frac{b}{|\sin \theta|} \\ & \because \quad \theta \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right), \\ & \therefore \quad|\cos \theta|=\cos \theta,|\sin \theta|=\sin \theta \end{aligned}$$

So,

$$\begin{aligned} & (0, T)\left(0, T_1\right)=\frac{a b}{\sin \theta \cos \theta}=\frac{2 a b}{2 \sin \theta \cos \theta}=\frac{2 a b}{\sin 2 \theta} \\ & \theta \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \Rightarrow 2 \theta \in(0, \pi) \Rightarrow \sin 2 \theta \in(0,1] \\ & \text { i.e., } \max .(\sin 2 \theta)=1 \\ & \therefore \min \left\{(0, T)\left(0, T_1\right)\right\}=2 a b \\ & 0<\theta<\frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the lines joining the focii of the ellipse $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$ where $$a > b$$, and an extremity of its minor axis is inclined at an angle 60$$^\circ$$, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
explanation:
$$\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$$

Let eccentricity be $$e$$.

$$\therefore S_1(a e, 0), S_2(-a e, 0), B_1(0, b)$$

Given, $$\angle B_1 S_2 S_1=60^{\circ}$$

$$\therefore$$ Slope of $$B_1 S_2=\tan 60^{\circ}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow & \frac{b-0}{0-(-a e)} & =\sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow & b & =a e \sqrt{3} \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Also, $$b^2=a^2\left(1-e^2\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad(a e)^2(3)=a^2\left(1-e^2\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3 e^2=1-e^2 \Rightarrow e^2=\frac{1}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad e=\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: AB is a variable chord of the ellipse $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$. If AB subtends a right angle at the origin O, then $${1 \over {O{A^2}}} + {1 \over {O{B^2}}}$$ equals to
explanation:
Suppose OB makes an angle $$\theta$$ with the positive direction of the X-axis, then OA makes an angle of $$\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}$$ with the positive direction of the X-axis.

Let $$OA=r_1$$ and $$OB=r_2$$ coordinates of A and B are $$(-r_1\sin\theta,r_1\cos\theta)$$ and $$(r_2\cos\theta,r_2\sin\theta)$$ respectively.

As B lies on the ellipse

$${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{r_2^2{{\cos }^2}\theta } \over {{a^2}}} + {{r_2^2{{\sin }^2}\theta } \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {O{B^2}}} = {1 \over {r_2^2}} = {{{{\cos }^2}\theta } \over {{a^2}}} + {{{{\sin }^2}\theta } \over {{b^2}}}$$ ..... (i)

Similarly, as A lies on the ellipse

$${1 \over {O{A^2}}} = {1 \over {r_1^2}} = {{{{\sin }^2}\theta } \over {{a^2}}} + {{{{\cos }^2}\theta } \over {{b^2}}}$$ ..... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$${1 \over {O{A^2}}} + {1 \over {O{B^2}}} = {1 \over {{a^2}}} + {1 \over {{b^2}}}$$

content: The co-ordinate of a point on the auxiliary circle of the ellipse x<sup>2</sup> + 2y<sup>2</sup> = 4 corresponding to the point on the ellipse whose eccentric angle is 60$$^\circ$$ will be
explanation:
Given, equation of ellipse x2 + 2y2 = 4

$$ \Rightarrow {{{x^2}} \over 4} + {{{y^2}} \over 2} = 1$$ ..... (i)


Now, equation of auxiliary circle is x2 + y2 = 4

$$\therefore$$ Point on the auxiliary circle with eccentric angle 60$$^\circ$$ is P(cos60$$^\circ$$, 2sin60$$^\circ$$)

i.e. $$P(1,\sqrt 3 )$$

content: The points of intersection of two ellipses $${x^2} + 2{y^2} - 6x - 12y + 20 = 0$$ and $$2{x^2} + {y^2} - 10x - 6y + 15 = 0$$ lie on a circle. The centre of the circle is
explanation:
Given ellipse,

$${x^2} + 2{y^2} - 6x - 12y + 20 = 0$$ and

$$2{x^2} + {y^2} - 10x - 6y + 15 = 0$$

The point of intersection of the given ellipse is

$$({x^2} + 2{y^2} - 6x - 12y + 20) + \lambda (2{x^2} + {y^2} - 10x - 6y + 15) = 0$$

$$(1 + 2\lambda ){x^2} + (2 + \lambda ){y^2} - (6 + 10\lambda )x - (12 + 6\lambda ) + 20 + 5\lambda = 0$$

The points lie on circle

$$\therefore$$ Equation becomes circle

$$\therefore$$ $$1 + 2\lambda = 2 + \lambda \Rightarrow \lambda = 1$$

$$\therefore$$ Equation of circle is

$$3{x^2} + 3{y^2} - 16x - 18y + 35 = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ Centre of circle is $$\left( {{8 \over 3},3} \right)$$

content: If B and B' are the ends of minor axis and S and S' are the foci of the ellipse $${{{x^2}} \over {25}} + {{{y^2}} \over 9} = 1$$, then the area of the rhombus SBS' B' will be
explanation:
Equation of ellipse

$${{{x^2}} \over {25}} + {{{y^2}} \over 9} = 1$$


Here, B = (0, 3), B' = (0, $$ - $$ 3)

S = (4, 0), S' = (0, $$ - $$ 4)

Area of rhombus SBS'B' = $${1 \over 2}SS' \times BB'$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} \times 8 \times 6 = 24$$ sq units

content: Consider the curve $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$. The portion of the tangent at any point of the curve intercepted between the point of contact and the directrix subtends at the corresponding focus an angle of
explanation: Since, the length of tangent between the point of contact and the point where it meets the directrix subtends right angle at the corresponding focus.

content: Consider the curve $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$. The portion of the tangent at any point of the curve intercepted between the point of contact and the directrix subtends at the corresponding focus an angle of
explanation: Since, the length of tangent between the point of contact and the point where it meets the directrix subtends right angle at the corresponding focus.

content: S and T are the foci of an ellipse and B is the end point of the minor axis. If STB is equilateral triangle, the eccentricity of the ellipse is
explanation:
Equation of the ellipse is

$${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$


Foci are S(ae, 0), T($$-$$ae, 0).

B(0, b) is the end of the minor axis.

STB is an equilateral triangle.

SB = ST

$$ \Rightarrow $$ SB2 = ST2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ a2e2 + b2 = 4a2e2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ b2 = 3a2e2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ a2(1 $$-$$ e2) = 3a2e2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 1 $$-$$ e2 = 3e2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 4e2 = 1

$$ \Rightarrow $$ e2 = $${1 \over 4}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$e = $${1 \over 2}$$

Eccentricity of the ellipse, e = $${1 \over 2}$$

content: P is the extremity of the latusrectum of ellipse $$3{x^2} + 4{y^2} = 48$$ in the first quadrant. The eccentric angle of P is
explanation:
Given equation of ellipse is

$$3{x^2} + 4{y^2} = 48$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{3{x^2}} \over {48}} + {{4{y^2}} \over {48}} = 1 \Rightarrow {{{x^2}} \over {16}} + {{{y^2}} \over {12}} = 1$$

Here, $$a = 4$$ and $$b = 2\sqrt 3 $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$e = \sqrt {1 - {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {1 - {{12} \over {16}}} = \sqrt {{4 \over {16}}} = \sqrt {{1 \over 4}} = {1 \over 2}$$

$$ \therefore $$ Coordinates of P are = (2, 3)

$$ \because $$ $$(4\cos \theta ,2\sqrt 3 \sin \theta ) = (2,3)$$ $$ \because $$ $$\left[ {p = \left( {ae,{{{b^2}} \over a}} \right)} \right]$$

By comparing, we get

$$4\cos \theta = 2$$ and $$2\sqrt 3 \sin \theta = 3$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = {1 \over 2}$$ and $$\sin \theta = {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \cos {\pi \over 3}$$ and $$\sin \theta = \sin {\pi \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \theta = {\pi \over 3}$$ and $$\theta = {\pi \over 3}$$

$$ \therefore $$ eccentric angle of P is $${\pi \over 3}$$.

content: Let P be a point on the ellipse $${{{x^2}} \over 9} + {{{y^2}} \over 4} = 1$$ and the line through P parallel to the Y-axis meets the circle x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> = 9 at Q, where P, Q are on the same side of the X-axis. If R is a point on PQ such that $${{PR} \over {RQ}} = {1 \over 2}$$, then the locus of R is
explanation:
Since, point P on the ellipse $${{{x^2}} \over 9} + {{{y^2}} \over 4} = 1$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$P(3cos\theta ,2sin\theta )$$

Given $${{PR} \over {RQ}} = {1 \over 2}$$



$$ \therefore $$ $$h = {{3\cos \theta + 6\cos \theta } \over 3},k = {{3\sin \theta + 4\sin \theta } \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow h = 3\cos \theta ,k = {7 \over 3}\sin \theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = h/3,\sin \theta = {{3k} \over 7}$$

Now, $${\cos ^2}\theta + {\sin ^2}\theta = {h^2}/9 + {{9{k^2}} \over {49}} = 1$$

Hence, locus of a point is $${{{x^2}} \over 9} + {{9{y^2}} \over {49}} = 1$$

content: B is an extremity of the minor axis of an ellipse whose foci are S and S'. If $$\angle SBS'$$ is a right angle, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
explanation:
Slope of SB, $${m_1} = {{b - 0} \over {0 - ae}} = - {b \over {ae}}$$ and slope of S'B, $${m_2} = {{b - 0} \over {0 - ( - ae)}} = {b \over {ae}}$$

Since, $$\angle SBS'$$ is a right angle.

$$ \therefore $$ $${m_1}{m_2} = - 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{ - b} \over {ae}} \times {b \over {ae}} = - 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {b^2} = {a^2}{e^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}} = {e^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 1 - {e^2} = {e^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{e^2} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^2} = {1 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow e = \pm {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow e = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$ or $$e = - {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$

content: Tangents are drawn to the ellipse  $${{{x^2}} \over 9} + {{{y^2}} \over 5} = 1$$ at the ends of both latusrectum. The area of the quadrilateral, so formed is
explanation:
We have,

$${{{x^2}} \over 9} + {{{y^2}} \over 5} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow a = 3$$ and $$b = \sqrt 5 $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$e = \sqrt {1 - {{{b^2}} \over {{a^2}}}} $$

$$ = \sqrt {1 - {5 \over 9}} = {2 \over 3}$$

$$ \therefore $$ Foci = ($$ \pm $$ ae, 0)

$$ = \left( { \pm 3 \times {2 \over 3},0} \right) = ( \pm 2,0)$$

$$ \therefore $$ Ends of latusrectum = $$\left( { \pm 2 \pm {5 \over 3}} \right)$$


Equation of tangent at the end of latusrectum P is

$${{x \times 2} \over 9} + {{y \times 5/3} \over 5} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {2 \over 9}x + {1 \over 3}y = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2x + 3y = 9$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$OA = {9 \over 2}$$ and $$OB = 3$$

$$ \therefore $$ Area of quadrilateral ABCD

= 4 $$ \times $$ Area of $$\Delta$$OAB

$$ = 4 \times {1 \over 2} \times OA \times OB$$

$$ = 4 \times {1 \over 2} \times {9 \over 2} \times 3$$

= 27 sq units.

content: The line y = x + $$\lambda$$ is tangent to the ellipse 2x<sup>2</sup> + 3y<sup>2</sup> = 1. Then, $$\lambda$$ is
explanation:
The equation of the line is y = x + $$\lambda$$ ....... (i)

On comparing it with y = mx + c, we get,

m = 1 and c = $$\lambda$$.

The equation of the ellipse is

2x2 + 3y2 = 1

or $${{{x^2}} \over {{{\left( {{1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)}^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{{\left( {{1 \over {\sqrt 3 }}} \right)}^2}}} = 1$$

On comparing it with $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$, we get

$${a^2} = {1 \over 2}$$ and $${b^2} = {1 \over 3}$$

If the line touches the ellipse, then

$${c^2} = {a^2}{m^2} + {b^2} \Rightarrow {\lambda ^2} = {1 \over 2}.1 + {1 \over 3} = {5 \over 6}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\lambda ^2} = {5 \over 6} \Rightarrow \lambda = \sqrt {{5 \over 6}} $$

content: The equation of auxiliary circle of the ellipse $$16{x^2} + 25{y^2} + 32x - 100y = 284$$ is
explanation:
Given equation of ellipse is $$16{x^2} + 25{y^2} + 32x - 100y = 284$$

Simplifying the equation, we have ellipse as

$${{{{(x + 1)}^2}} \over {25}} + {{{{(y - 2)}^2}} \over {16}} = 1$$

So, the auxiliary equation of circle is

$${(x + 1)^2} + {(y - 2)^2} = 25$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} + 2x + 1{ + ^2} - 4y + 4 = 25$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${x^2} + {y^2} + 2x - 4y - 20 = 0$$

content: The points of the ellipse 16x<sup>2</sup> + 9y<sup>2</sup> = 400 at which the ordinate decreases at the same rate at which the abscissa increases is/are given by
explanation:
Given that, the ordinate decreases at the same rate at which the abscissa increases, therefore

$${{dy} \over {dt}} = - {{dx} \over {dt}}$$ .... (i)

Also equation of ellipse

$$16{x^2} + 9{y^2} = 400$$ .... (ii)

On differentiating w.r.t. t, we get

$$16 \times 2x{{dx} \over {dt}} + 9 \times 2y{{dy} \over {dt}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16x{{dx} \over {dt}} + 9y{{dy} \over {dt}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 9y{{dy} \over {dt}} = - 16x{{dx} \over {dt}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 9y{{dy} \over {dt}} = - 16x\left( { - {{dy} \over {dt}}} \right)$$ [using Eq. (i)]

$$ \Rightarrow 9y = 16x \Rightarrow y = {{16} \over 9}x$$ .... (iii)

Using Eq. (iii) in Eq. (ii), we get

$$16{x^2} + 9{\left( {{{16} \over 9}x} \right)^2} = 400$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16{x^2} + {{{{(16)}^2}} \over 9}{x^2} = 400$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16x\left[ {x + {{16} \over 9}x} \right] = 400$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16x\left[ {{{25} \over 9}x} \right] = 400 \Rightarrow {x^2}{{400 \times 9} \over {25 \times 16}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} = 9 \Rightarrow x = \pm 3$$

$$ \therefore $$ Required points are $$\left( {3,{{16} \over 3}} \right)$$ and $$\left( { - 3, - {{16} \over 3}} \right)$$

content: Let f be a strictly decreasing function defined on R such that $$f(x) > 0,\forall x \in R$$. Let $${{{x^2}} \over {f({a^2} + 5a + 3)}} + {{{y^2}} \over {f(a + 15)}} = 1$$ be an ellipse with major axis along the y-axis. The value of 'a' can lie in the interval (s)
explanation:
If $$f(x)$$ is an decreasing function, then

$$\begin{aligned} \text{for} \quad & x>y \Rightarrow f(x)< f(y) \\ & f\left(a^2+5 a+\right. \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad a^2+4 a-12>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(a+6)(a-2)>0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a \in(-\infty,-6) \cup(2, \infty) \end{aligned}$$

content: Chords of an ellipse are drawn through the positive end of the minor axis. Their midpoint lies on
explanation:
Let (h, k) be the mid-point of a chord passing through the positive end of the minor axis of the ellipse $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

Then, the equation of the chord is

$${{hx} \over {{a^2}}} + {{ky} \over {{b^2}}} - 1 = {{{h^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{k^2}} \over {{b^2}}} - 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{hx} \over {{a^2}}} + {{ky} \over {{b^2}}} = {{{h^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{k^2}} \over {{b^2}}}$$

Thus, curve passes through the minor axis (0, b).

$$\therefore$$ $${k \over b} = {{{h^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{k^2}} \over {{b^2}}}$$.

Hence, the locus of (h, k) is $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} + {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = {y \over b}$$, which represent an ellipse.

content: Consider a tangent to the ellipse $${{{x^2}} \over 2} + {{{y^2}} \over 1} = 1$$ at any point. The locus of the mid-point of the portion intercepted between the axes is
explanation:
Equation of tangent to the ellipse $${{{x^2}} \over 2} + {{{y^2}} \over 1} = 1$$ at point (x1, y1) is

$${{x{x_{^2}}} \over 2} + {{y{y_{^2}}} \over 1} = 1$$ ....(i)

Let mid-point of intercept be P(h, k).

$$ \therefore $$ $$h = {1 \over {{x_1}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x_1} = {1 \over h}$$

and $$k = {1 \over {2{y_1}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y_1} = {1 \over {2k}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ Required locus of the mid-point

$$ = {1 \over {2{x^2}}} + {1 \over {4{y^2}}} = 1$$

content: On the ellipse 4x<sup>2</sup> + 9y<sup>2</sup> = 1, the points at which the tangents are parallel to the line 8x = 9y, are
explanation:
Given equation of ellipse is

4x2 + 9y2 = 1 ... (i)

On differentiating, we get

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 8x + 18yy' = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$y' = {{ - 8x} \over {18y}} = m$$ .... (ii)

Also, 8x = 9y [equation of line]

On differentiating, we get

$$ \Rightarrow $$8 = 9y'

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$y' = {{ - 8} \over 9} = m$$ .... (iii)

[$$ \because $$ tangents are parallel to this line]

So, by Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get,

$${{ - 8x} \over {18y}} = {8 \over 9}$$

$$ \Rightarrow - x = 2y$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = - 2y$$

On substituting $$ \Rightarrow x = - 2y$$ in Eq. (i), we get

$$4{( - 2y)^2} + 9{y^2} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow 16{y^2} + 9{y^2} = 1 \Rightarrow 25{y^2} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = \pm {1 \over 5}$$ $$ \therefore $$ $$x = \mp {2 \over 5}$$

So, required points are $$\left( {{2 \over 5}, - {1 \over 5}} \right)$$ and $$\left( { - {2 \over 5},{1 \over 5}} \right)$$
